---
title: "[Punchy Title]: [The Concept or Tension at Stake]"
subtitle: "[A short, human framing line] — a COMP 1150 case study"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
# Format, bibliography, and styling are inherited from _quarto.yml
# (HTML + DOCX, refs.bib, _case-styles.scss). Override here only if a
# single case needs something special.
---

<!--
====================================================================
COMP 1150 CASE STUDY TEMPLATE  (Summer 2026)
Read case_study_style_guide.md before drafting. Quick reminders:

  - ~3,000 words of core prose (The Case + the argument dialogue);
    ~5,000 all-in once context boxes, figure, questions, refs are added.
  - Pairs with exactly one notebook (see course_outline.md table).
  - Three jobs, all required:
      (1) tell the story;
      (2) make the reader UNDERSTAND & APPLY the core CS concept(s) —
          reinforcing material they are encountering elsewhere in the
          course, WITHOUT NAMING where ("no NB 4," no "as in lecture");
      (3) open a real ethics/governance/practice debate.
  - "How It Worked" carries job (2). Short Python snippets (5–25
    lines, runnable) are welcome wherever the reader benefits from
    seeing the concept work.
  - The argument section carries job (3). Use 2–3 formal .argument
    blocks for the central tension; demote other positions to labeled
    prose so it does not read as a philosophy paper.
  - Attribution: Brendan Shea, PhD. Never the college.
  - Delete every HTML comment before publishing.

Custom callout classes used below (.case-at-a-glance, .context-box,
.argument) need styling. A starter _case-styles.scss is referenced in
the style guide; until it exists they render as plain divs, which is
fine for drafting.
====================================================================
-->

::: {.case-at-a-glance}
- **Who:** [The named people / system / organization at the center. Concrete.]
- **What:** [One sentence: the event, decision, or artifact and what went wrong or was at stake.]
- **Where / When:** [Place(s) and dates. Be specific enough to ground the story.]
- **Why it matters:** [Why this is *the* canonical example of the tension. Name the two
  forces that collide using [key-term]{.key-term} spans — e.g.
  [correctness]{.key-term} vs. [delivery pressure]{.key-term}.]
- **Concepts at play:** [list the core CS concepts the case turns on
  — the things the reader will leave more fluent in. No notebook or
  lecture references.]
:::

## The Case

<!--
The narrative. ~1,200–1,500 words. Tell it like the Dax case: real
people doing recognizable things, present-tense stakes, no editorializing
yet. The reader should care before any argument starts. Plant — but do
not yet resolve — the technical facts the later arguments will turn on.

Use .context-box callouts to teach the core CS concept exactly when the
story needs it, so the CS content is load-bearing, not bolted on. Save
the deeper "how it actually works" walk-through for the "How It Worked"
section below. Bold new technical terms and mark them with
[term]{.key-term} for the glossary / styling. Never reference notebooks,
lectures, or the course.
-->

[Open in a scene. Establish the people and the system. Build to the
decision or failure.]

::: {.context-box}
**[The core CS concept, explained here because the story needs it.]**
[2–5 sentences. Define each [technical term]{.key-term} in plain
language. Tie it to what the reader just saw happen. Keep it tight —
the longer walk-through belongs in "How It Worked" below.]
:::

[Continue the narrative. What was tried, who objected, what the
institution did. End the section on the unresolved question — not a
verdict. State plainly that the *outcome* is not the interesting part;
*whose call it was / what we should have known / what we owed* is.]

## How It Worked

<!--
The CS-at-play section. ~500–800 words.

This is where the reader gets fluent in the core technical concept the
case turns on — not by being told history, but by walking through the
mechanism and (where it helps) running a small piece of code. Job (2)
of the three jobs lives here.

  - Walk through the technical mechanism step by step.
  - Include 0–3 short Python snippets (5–25 lines, runnable) when the
    reader benefits from seeing the concept work — a tiny demo of the
    bug, the algorithm, the data structure, the overflow. Comment the
    snippet sparingly; let the prose do the explaining.
  - Prose-only is acceptable when code would be contrived (e.g., a
    case about geopolitics or governance).
  - Use [key-term]{.key-term} spans on terms first introduced here.
  - Do NOT reference notebooks, lectures, or the course.
-->

[Walk the reader through the mechanism in plain language. State what
the system was supposed to do, what it actually did, and where the
[concept]{.key-term} entered.]

```python
# A small, runnable demonstration of the concept at the heart of the
# case. Keep it short. Output something the reader can predict and
# then check.
```

[Brief explanation of what the snippet shows and why it matches the
real-world failure / decision / behavior in the case. If a second
snippet helps — e.g., the buggy version vs. the fixed version — add
it. If not, stop.]

## The Argument [X] Started

<!--
The ethics/governance spine. ~1,000–1,500 words.

A *connected* dialogue. Reconstruct real positions (engineers,
historians, ethicists, courts, companies) — steelman every side. Aim
for 2–4 linked moves total, with at most 2–3 in formal .argument
blocks; the rest are prose paragraphs with a short bold label
("**The accountability objection.**"). Stacking four or five formal
blocks is the philosophy-paper shape this template is deliberately
avoiding.

Whatever the shape, each move must say what it challenges in the
prior one. End on the current-practice / empirical turn and a sharper
question.
-->

### [The opening argument — a short heading naming the move]

[1–2 paragraphs of setup: whose position this is and in their own words
(quote + @citekey where possible).]

::: {.argument}
**The [Name] Argument**

1. [Premise 1.]
2. [Premise 2 — usually the one the reply will attack.]
3. Therefore, [conclusion].
:::

[Say which premise carries the weight, and hand off to the reply.]

::: {.context-box}
**[Background concept needed to judge this argument.]**
[Define [terms]{.key-term}. Keep it tight. Cite history/standards with
@citekey where relevant — e.g. when a standard or technique was actually
new, so the reader judges the actors by what they could have known.]
:::

### [The reply — names the premise it denies]

[Setup prose: this side does not deny premise 1; it denies premise 2,
because…]

::: {.argument}
**The [Name] Reply**

1. [Premise.]
2. [Premise.]
3. [Premise.]
4. Therefore, [conclusion].
:::

[Name the soft spot in the reply — the move the next block exploits.]

### [The counter]

::: {.argument}
**The [Name] Objection**

1. [Premise.]
2. [Premise.]
3. Therefore, [conclusion].
:::

<!-- Add a reframing block and/or the empirical-turn block as the case
demands. Keep each tied to the one before. -->

### [The argument that hasn't ended: the current-practice turn]

[Where does this rest *today*? Use current evidence: a recent incident,
a standard that did/didn't get adopted, what industry actually does now
vs. what the tidy lesson says. Close on the sharper question, not a
resolution.]

<!--
OPTIONAL FIGURE. Use a {dot} graphviz block when a decision/process is
the cleanest way to show a position (cf. the Arnold–Menzel diagram).
All figures generated by code, fully labeled, with a fig-cap that
explains how to read it. Delete this block if unused.
-->

```{dot}
//| label: fig-decision
//| fig-cap: "[One sentence saying what the diagram shows and how to read it — the gold node is the pivot, the green node is the endpoint, etc. Cite the source it is adapted from.]"
digraph G {
  rankdir=TB;
  node [shape=box, style="rounded,filled", fillcolor="#eef3f8",
        fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=11, margin="0.18,0.10"];
  edge [fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=10];

  start [label="[Starting situation]", fillcolor="#dde8f0"];
  pivot [label="[The decisive question]", shape=diamond, fillcolor="#f3e9d8"];
  endp  [label="[The endpoint]", fillcolor="#e3efe1"];

  start -> pivot;
  pivot -> endp [label="  [condition]"];
}
```

## Discussion Questions

<!--
5 questions. Mix technical and ethical — at least 2 of each. Critical-
thinking outcome: students must evaluate competing claims, not recall
answers. Required coverage:
  - ≥1 makes them WORK THE CS (modify a snippet, predict output,
    apply the concept to a fresh scenario).
  - ≥1 makes them reconstruct two positions and name what they
    actually disagree about.
  - ≥1 puts them inside the scenario as a decision-maker.
  - ≥1 applies the case's framework or technique to a second
    contrasting case.
No question should have a single correct answer. No question should
mention notebooks, lectures, or the course.
-->

<!--
Keep each question SHORT. Plain words, short sentences, one main idea
per sentence. Many students read in their second language. A good
question is 1–3 short sentences. Cut everything else.
-->

1. Run the snippet from "How It Worked." Change [one thing] so [outcome].
   What does your version print?
2. Pick one design choice that would have stopped the failure. Sketch it
   in a few lines of pseudocode. Why this one?
3. Write **The [X] Argument** and **The [Y] Reply** in your own words.
   What is the one thing they really disagree about? What evidence could
   settle it?
4. You are [a specific role] at the moment [the decision]. What do you do?
   Why? What does your answer say about a system that puts this choice on you?
5. Pick one modern case: [example A], [example B], or [example C]. Who is
   responsible? Is your answer easier or harder than for this case?

## Further Reading

<!-- 3–5 entries: the documentaries / primary reports / key essays /
authoritative journalism. Each with an @citekey that exists in refs.bib. -->

- [Primary source — report, paper, or documentary] [@citekey].
- [Key secondary analysis] [@citekey].
- [Accessible journalism or retrospective] [@citekey].

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
